ace_test_project_guide.overview:
This guide covers everything required to create an IBM App Connect Enterprise (ACE) v13 Test Project that tests message flows using the ACE Integration Test Framework (JUnit 5 + NodeSpy API). It documents the correct project structure, required files, Java test patterns, and how to generate tests from recorded messages (.mxml files).
ACE Test Projects are the standard way to write automated unit and integration tests for ACE message flows directly inside the ACE Toolkit. They use the com.ibm.integration.test.v1 library (Integration Test Library) and JUnit 5.
From IBM documentation (https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/app-connect/13.0.x?topic=dit-generating-tests-from-recorded-messages-in-message-flow): "You can configure an integration server to store message assembly files in a specified directory for every message that is processed by a message flow. The test project contains a test case for every node in a message flow, apart from an input node. Each test case uses a message assembly file for input to the node and another message assembly to do a comparison check."
IBM-defined procedure (Record → Generate → Compile → [Import] → Run): 1. Configure an integration server to record messages 2. Generate a test project by using the ibmint generate tests command 3. Compile the test project and create a BAR file by using the command line 4. (Optional) Import the test project into the Toolkit for editing 5. (Optional) Run the tests using the command line or Toolkit

absolute_rule.title: DO NOT modify or mock recorded .mxml files without explicit user confirmation
absolute_rule.rule:
Recorded message files (.mxml) are immutable runtime artefacts captured by the ACE RecordedMessageManager. They represent the ground truth of what the flow actually produced at runtime. You MUST NOT:
- Edit, rewrite, or patch any .mxml file - Replace a recorded .mxml with a synthetic/hand-crafted substitute - Alter field values, checkpoint metadata, or any content inside an .mxml file - Delete or rename recorded .mxml files
unless the user has EXPLICITLY asked you to do so OR has explicitly confirmed it when you have asked them first.
Hand-crafted .mxml files (created from scratch for unrecorded paths such as error scenarios) are permitted WITHOUT confirmation because they are new files, not modifications to existing recordings. Always clearly label hand-crafted files with a distinct name (e.g. getCurrentWeather_handleError_input.mxml) so they are never confused with genuine recordings.
If you believe a recorded .mxml needs to be changed for a test to work, STOP and ask the user to confirm before touching the file. Explain why the change is needed and propose the minimal edit as a diff for review.
absolute_rule.rationale: Recorded messages are the single source of truth for what the application produced under real conditions. Silently modifying them would corrupt the regression baseline and invalidate the purpose of the test suite.
absolute_rule.permitted_without_confirmation: - COPYING recorded .mxml files from RecordedMessages directory to test resources - READING recorded .mxml files to analyse checkpoints or generate the message index - CREATING brand-new hand-crafted .mxml files for paths with no recordings
absolute_rule.requires_explicit_confirmation: - Editing any field value inside an existing recorded .mxml - Removing a checkpoint element or changing source/target metadata - Replacing a recorded .mxml with a different file - Deleting a recorded .mxml

recorded_messages.description: The ACE RecordedMessageManager captures message assemblies at every node transition in a running flow. Each captured checkpoint is saved as an .mxml file. These files are the PREFERRED source of test data because they contain real runtime message trees.

how_to_enable_recording.description: Enable recording in the integration server overrides/server.conf.yaml

file_naming_convention.description:
Recorded files follow the naming convention: [messageId]-[propagationCount].mxml
The propagationCount is a sequential integer starting at 0 for the input node. Each subsequent node transition increments it by 1.
file_naming_convention.example: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-0.mxml ← propagationCount=0: HTTP Input → out (the trigger) 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-1.mxml ← propagationCount=1: Compute → out 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-2.mxml ← propagationCount=2: HTTP Request → out (backend response)

checkpoint_structure.description: Each .mxml file contains a MessageAssembly with a checkpoint section that identifies: - messageId: groups all checkpoints for the same message invocation - propagationCount: sequential position in the flow - source: the node and terminal that EMITTED this message - target: the node and terminal that will RECEIVE this message - message: the full message tree at this point - localEnvironment: LocalEnvironment tree at this point - environment: Environment tree at this point - exceptionList: any exceptions at this point
checkpoint_structure.how_to_read_checkpoint: To understand which node a checkpoint belongs to: - The checkpoint SOURCE tells you WHICH NODE produced the message and via WHICH TERMINAL - The checkpoint TARGET tells you which node will receive it - Use SOURCE to identify the node under test: the input is the PREVIOUS checkpoint, the output is THIS checkpoint

mapping_to_tests.description:
Map recorded checkpoints to node-level tests as follows: For each node N in the flow: - INPUT to node N = the .mxml where TARGET.node = N (i.e., propagationCount = N's position - 1) - OUTPUT of node N = the .mxml where SOURCE.node = N (i.e., propagationCount = N's position)
Example for a flow: HTTP Input(0) → Prepare Request(1) → HTTP Request(2) → HTTP Reply Node "Prepare Request": input = checkpoint -0 (SOURCE=HTTP Input, TARGET=Prepare Request) output = checkpoint -1 (SOURCE=Prepare Request, TARGET=HTTP Request) Node "HTTP Request": input = checkpoint -1 (SOURCE=Prepare Request, TARGET=HTTP Request) output = checkpoint -2 (SOURCE=HTTP Request, TARGET=HTTP Reply)

recorded_messages.where_to_place_mxml_files: Copy the .mxml files from the RecordedMessageManager output directory into: [AppName]_Test/src/main/resources/ They are loaded at runtime via the classloader as classpath resources.

java_test_patterns.description: All test classes use JUnit 5 (@Test, @AfterEach, @DisplayName) and the ACE com.ibm.integration.test.v1 library. The core pattern is NodeSpy.evaluate().

mandatory_cleanup.description: EVERY test class MUST have an @AfterEach method that calls TestSetup.restoreAllMocks(). This prevents mock state leaking between tests.

helper_method_load_assembly.description: Always include this private helper for loading .mxml files from the classpath. Use it in every test to load both input and expected assemblies.

pattern.name: node_test_from_recorded_messages
pattern.description: Standard pattern for a node-level test using recorded .mxml files. Replace placeholders: [APP] = application name (e.g. "WeatherAPI_App") [FLOW] = message flow name (e.g. "getCurrentWeather") [NODE] = node label as shown in the flow canvas (e.g. "Prepare Request") [INPUT] = resource path to the input .mxml (the checkpoint BEFORE this node) [EXPECTED] = resource path to the expected output .mxml (the checkpoint FROM this node) [TERMINAL] = output terminal name the node propagates to (e.g. "out", "failure", "out1")

pattern.name: node_test_with_ignore_paths
pattern.description: When the node makes outbound calls (e.g. HTTP Request node) or produces time-varying output, use ignorePath() to exclude fields that legitimately differ from the recording. Common fields to ignore for HTTP Request nodes: /Message/BLOB/BLOB — response body (content changes over time) /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/Date — HTTP date header /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-CachedAt — CDN timestamp /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-RequestId — unique per request /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-Cache — cache hit/miss varies /Message/HTTPResponseHeader/x-weatherapi-qpm-left — rate limit counter /LocalEnvironment/WrittenDestination — contains request timestamps
pattern.code: assertThat(actualOutput, equalsMessage(expectedOutput) .ignorePath("/Message/BLOB/BLOB", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/Date", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-CachedAt", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-RequestId", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/CDN-Cache", false) .ignorePath("/Message/HTTPResponseHeader/x-weatherapi-qpm-left", false) .ignorePath("/LocalEnvironment/WrittenDestination", true) );
pattern.ignorepath_syntax: ignorePath(path, recursive) path = "/" separated path into the message tree recursive = true → ignore the entire subtree rooted at path recursive = false → ignore only the leaf value at path

pattern.name: subflow_node_reference
pattern.description: For nodes INSIDE a subflow, use .subflowNode() before .node() in the SpyObjectReference.
pattern.code: SpyObjectReference nodeRef = new SpyObjectReference() .application("[APP]") .messageFlow("[FLOW]") .subflowNode("[SubflowNodeLabel]") // label of the subflow instance in the parent flow .node("[NodeInsideSubflow]"); // label of the node inside the subflow

pattern.name: terminal_assert_only
pattern.description: When you only want to check that a node propagated to the correct terminal (without comparing the full message tree), omit the equalsMessage assertion. Useful for quick smoke tests or when the output is non-deterministic.

pattern.name: mock_downstream_node
pattern.description: When a node makes an outbound call you want to prevent (e.g. HTTP Request to a real backend), use NodeSpy.setStopAtInputTerminal("in") on the downstream node to intercept the message before it enters and executes that node. This makes the test fully offline / hermetic.

step.title: Identify the Application under test
step.description: Note the exact application project name. The test project name follows the convention GenTest_[Version]_[AppName] (e.g. GenTest_0_WeatherAPI_App). See naming_conventions section.

step.title: Locate recorded messages
step.description:
Check whether .mxml recorded messages exist. Look in: - Any directory configured in RecordedMessageManager.recordedMessagePath - Subdirectory structure is: [recordedMessagePath]/[AppName]/[FlowName]/[messageId]-[n].mxml
If recorded messages exist: use them (preferred — real runtime data). If NOT: do NOT attempt to write .mxml files by hand. Record real messages by enabling RecordedMessageManager in server.conf.yaml and invoking the flow. Hand-writing .mxml files is inadvisable — the iib: namespace schema is complex and hand-crafted files are fragile and hard to maintain.
ibmint generate tests searches ALL subdirectories of --recorded-messages recursively. You can pass any level — a per-flow subdir, the app-level dir, or any parent. The IBM docs primary example uses a per-flow directory (--recorded-messages flow1). Pass the app-level directory to generate tests covering all flows in one go.

step.title: Generate message index to understand coverage
step.bob_instruction:
WHENEVER the user asks for the message index OR asks to analyse recorded messages, you MUST run ibmint generate msgindex — do NOT manually parse the .mxml files.
When this step is reached during test project creation, run ibmint generate msgindex against the recordings directory. This tells you exactly which paths are covered before generating tests — so you can inform the user of any coverage gaps BEFORE generating the test project.
IMPORTANT: ibmint requires the ACE profile to be loaded first. The profile sets PATH, MQSI_BASE_FILEPATH, and all required environment variables. ibmint will not be found on PATH without it.
step.why_its_useful: - Shows EXACTLY which nodes were exercised by the recordings - Directly tells you the (input .mxml, expected output .mxml) pair per node - Identifies coverage gaps (nodes with no checkpoint pair) BEFORE generating tests - Prevents incorrect checkpoint pairings that cause false test failures - The format [NodeLabel]: input; output maps 1:1 to test case structure

execution_commands.windows:
CRITICAL: On Windows, ibmint is not on PATH until mqsiprofile.cmd is sourced. PowerShell cannot source a .cmd file directly. Use cmd /c with both commands chained so the profile environment is inherited by ibmint in the same shell:
$cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages `"$cwd\[path-to-recordings]`"" 2>&1
Key points: - Use backtick-escaped double quotes around the mqsiprofile.cmd path (has spaces) - Use && to chain: profile must succeed before ibmint runs - Wrap the recordings path in quotes if it contains spaces - ACE version in the path (13.0.8.0) may differ — check C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\ - Add 2>&1 to capture both stdout and stderr (BIP messages go to stderr)
Concrete example (WeatherAPI — top-level app recordings dir): $cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages `"$cwd\WeatherAPI_App_RecordedMessages_Orig\WeatherAPI_App`"" 2>&1
execution_commands.linux_aix:
Source the profile then run ibmint in the same shell session:
source /opt/ibm/ace/13.0.8.0/server/bin/mqsiprofile ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages /path/to/recordings/AppName/

step.reading_the_output:
Each output block covers one MessageID (one recorded invocation). Within each block, each line maps directly to one test case: NodeLabel: input.mxml; output.mxml
A node with no input file (e.g. "HTTP Input: ; -0.mxml") is an input-type node — it triggers the flow but is not directly testable via NodeSpy.
A node with no output file (e.g. "HTTP Reply: -2.mxml;") is a terminal node — messages arrive here but it does not propagate further.
Example ibmint output: MessageID: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001 HTTP Input: ; 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-0.mxml Prepare Request: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-0.mxml; 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-1.mxml HTTP Request: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-1.mxml; 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-2.mxml HTTP Reply: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-2.mxml;
Means: Test "Prepare Request": input=-0.mxml expected=-1.mxml terminal=out Test "HTTP Request": input=-1.mxml expected=-2.mxml terminal=out (live call — use ignorePath)
A session missing a node means that path was NOT exercised in that invocation. Example — missing "HTTP Request" line means failure terminal fired (400 path): MessageID: 00008E3C-6A4EB973-00000002 HTTP Input: ; 00008E3C-6A4EB973-00000002-0.mxml Prepare Request: 00008E3C-6A4EB973-00000002-0.mxml; 00008E3C-6A4EB973-00000002-1.mxml HTTP Reply: 00008E3C-6A4EB973-00000002-1.mxml; → "Prepare Request" propagated via failure terminal (no HTTP Request in this session)
step.coverage_gap_detection:
After running the index, scan ALL sessions for nodes that NEVER appear as a source. Any node defined in the msgflow that never appears as a source in ANY session has zero recorded output — it was never exercised.
The correct fix for a coverage gap is to RECORD MORE MESSAGES: 1. Configure the scenario that exercises the missing path (e.g. use an invalid API key to trigger a backend failure, triggering the Handle Error path). 2. Invoke the flow with that scenario while recording is enabled. 3. Use the newly captured .mxml files when generating the test project.
Do NOT hand-write .mxml files to fill coverage gaps. Hand-crafted files are inadvisable — the iib: namespace schema is complex, values must exactly match what ACE would produce at runtime, and the files are fragile to maintain.

step.title: Generate the test project using ibmint generate tests (PRIMARY PATH)
step.bob_instruction:
ALWAYS use ibmint generate tests as the FIRST approach to creating a test project. Do NOT manually create the project structure, .project file, build.gradle, or Java test class from scratch. ibmint generate tests scaffolds all of this automatically from the recorded messages — and the resulting project is guaranteed to be structurally correct and importable into the ACE Toolkit.
Manual project creation (old Steps 5+6) is the FALLBACK only when: - ibmint is not available (ACE version below 12.0.8) - No recorded messages exist at all
After generating, the workflow continues at Step 5 (review and enhance).
step.description:
Run ibmint generate tests against a recordings directory. ibmint will: - Recursively search all subdirectories of --recorded-messages for .mxml files - Create a complete Gradle-based test project structure - Generate a Java test class with one test method per node transition per session - Copy all .mxml files into src/main/resources/ - Wire up testproject.descriptor referencing the application under test
PREREQUISITE: Gradle 8.2.1 or higher must be installed separately. ibmint generate tests generates a Gradle build (build.gradle), NOT a Maven pom.xml. Install Gradle from https://gradle.org/install/ before running this command.
All three parameters (--recorded-messages, --output-test-project, --java-class) are REQUIRED. There are no optional parameters except --help.
--recorded-messages is recursive: you can pass any directory level (app-level, flow-level, or any parent). ibmint walks all subdirectories automatically. Passing a flow-level dir generates tests only for that flow. Passing an app-level dir generates tests for all flows in that app.

execution_commands.windows:
Run via cmd /c so the ACE profile is active in the same shell:
$cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate tests --recorded-messages `"$cwd\[recordingsDir]`" --output-test-project `"$cwd\GenTest_0_[AppName]`" --java-class com.[org].[app].test.[ClassName]" 2>&1
Concrete example (WeatherAPI — all flows, app-level dir): $cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate tests --recorded-messages `"$cwd\WeatherAPI_App_RecordedMessages_Orig\WeatherAPI_App`" --output-test-project `"$cwd\GenTest_0_WeatherAPI_App`" --java-class com.weather.api.test.WeatherAPITest" 2>&1
Key points: - --recorded-messages is searched recursively — pass any level that contains .mxml files - --output-test-project is where the new Gradle project will be created - ACE version in the profile path may differ — check C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\ - Add 2>&1 to capture both stdout and stderr (BIP messages go to stderr) - Gradle must be installed separately (not bundled with ACE)

step.what_is_generated:
ibmint generate tests creates a Gradle-based project: GenTest_0_AppName/ ├── .project ← Eclipse project with ACE test natures ├── testproject.descriptor ← References the application under test ├── .classpath ← ACE test framework JARs on classpath ├── .settings/ │ └── org.eclipse.core.resources.prefs ├── build.gradle ← Gradle build descriptor (NOT pom.xml) ├── settings.gradle └── src/main/ ├── java/[package]/[ClassName].java ← Generated JUnit 5 test class └── resources/ └── *.mxml ← Copied from recorded messages
The generated Java class contains: - One @Test method per node transition per recorded session - NodeSpy.evaluate() calls with the correct -0, -1, -2 checkpoint files - equalsMessage() assertions for each transition - @AfterEach TestSetup.restoreAllMocks() cleanup
step.after_generation: After ibmint generate tests completes: 1. Review the generated Java class — it is a starting point, not a final test suite 2. Proceed to Step 5 to enhance the generated tests 3. Import into the ACE Toolkit: File → Import → Existing Projects into Workspace (the Toolkit auto-detects it as a Test Project from testproject.descriptor)

step.title: Review and enhance the generated tests
step.description: ibmint generate tests produces a mechanically correct but intentionally minimal test suite. The generated tests verify exact message equality for every node transition. Before using the test project for regression testing, review and enhance:

enhancements.enhancement: Add ignorePath() calls on HTTP Request tests for all time-varying fields. Generated tests will fail on the next run if the response body, Date header, CDN headers, or rate-limit counters change. See common_failures/timestamp_mismatch.
Rename test methods from generated identifiers (e.g. test_0_PrepareRequest_out_0) to descriptive names that explain the scenario under test.
Add comments explaining which PATH (A/B/C) each test exercises, and which session ID the recordings came from. Makes maintenance far easier.

For unrecorded paths (coverage gaps identified in Step 3), record additional messages rather than writing hand-crafted .mxml files. Re-run ibmint generate tests after recording to include the new paths automatically.
Group related test methods into @Nested inner classes (one per flow or one per path) to improve test output readability in CI/CD pipelines.

row.assertion: equalsMessage(expected) — exact match against recorded output
equalsMessage with ignorePath() for time-varying fields, OR setStopAtInputTerminal for offline

row.test_type: Not directly testable via NodeSpy (it IS the trigger)
row.assertion: Use the HTTP Input checkpoint (-0.mxml) as INPUT to the first Compute node test

row.test_type: Not directly testable via NodeSpy (terminal node)
row.assertion: Verify upstream node propagates to correct terminal

step.title: FALLBACK ONLY: Manually create the test project structure
step.description: Use this step ONLY when ibmint generate tests is unavailable (ACE below v12.0.8) or when no recorded messages exist. If you used Step 4 successfully, skip this step.

actions.action: Create [AppName]_Test/.project (use ACE toolkit natures — see project_structure section)
Write the Java test class manually using patterns from Section 4

mistake.problem: Using wrong .project natures — "Unknown referenced nature" warning
mistake.symptom: ACE Toolkit shows: "Unknown referenced nature: org.eclipse.buildship.core.gradleprojectnature" This happens because the Buildship (Eclipse Gradle) plugin is not installed in the ACE Toolkit.
mistake.solution:
Use ALL four ACE Toolkit natures in the .project file: com.ibm.etools.msgbroker.tooling.testProjectNature org.eclipse.jdt.core.javanature com.ibm.etools.mft.jcn.jcnnature com.ibm.etools.mft.bar.ext.barnature With the full builder chain starting with: org.eclipse.jdt.core.javabuilder (NOT mft.java.builder.javabuilder) com.ibm.etools.mft.applib.mbprojectbuilder ... (14 builders total — see the .project template in project_structure section)
Do NOT include org.eclipse.buildship.core.gradleprojectnature or org.eclipse.buildship.core.gradleprojectbuilder in the .project file. The build.gradle and settings.gradle files can still exist for command-line Gradle builds — only the .project natures/builders need to be ACE-only.

mistake.symptom: Tests pass individually but fail when run as a suite due to leaked mock state
mistake.solution: Always include the @AfterEach cleanup method in every test class

mistake.problem: Wrong .mxml file pairing for node input/output
mistake.symptom: equalsMessage assertion fails immediately — trees don't match at all
mistake.solution: Re-read the checkpoint metadata: the SOURCE of the output checkpoint identifies the node that produced it. The TARGET of the input checkpoint identifies the node that receives it. Ensure both point to the same node label.

mistake.problem: Not ignoring time-varying fields for HTTP Request tests
mistake.symptom: Test passes on the day of recording but fails the next day (timestamps differ)
mistake.solution: Add ignorePath() for Date headers, CDN fields, BLOB content (if it contains live data), and WrittenDestination timestamps. See Section 4 pattern "node_test_with_ignore_paths".

mistake.symptom: TestException at runtime: node not found
mistake.solution: The node label in .node("...") must exactly match the translation string in the .msgflow file (the string="..." attribute on the translation element). Example: string="Prepare Request" → .node("Prepare Request")

mistake.symptom: TestException: failed to parse message assembly — or stream returns null
mistake.solution: 1. Verify the .mxml file is in src/main/resources/ and the resource name passed to getResourceAsStream() matches the filename exactly (case-sensitive). 2. Verify the .mxml file is a genuine recording from RecordedMessageManager, not a manually edited or constructed file. The iib: namespace schema is strict — the root element requires iib:parser="GENERICROOT" and the message element requires iib:injectable="true". Any structural deviation causes parse failures. 3. If the file was copied rather than recorded fresh, confirm it was not accidentally modified. Compare it against the original in the RecordedMessages directory.
mistake.note: If no recording exists for the path you need to test, the correct fix is to record real messages for that scenario — not to write .mxml files by hand.

complete_example.description: Complete example based on WeatherAPI_App with 3 recorded checkpoints for getCurrentWeather. Recording: curl GET http://localhost:7800/weather/current?location=London Files: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-{0,1,2}.mxml
complete_example.checkpoint_analysis:
-0.mxml SOURCE=HTTP Input→out TARGET=Prepare Request→in (trigger message) -1.mxml SOURCE=Prepare Request→out TARGET=HTTP Request→in (ESQL output, URL built) -2.mxml SOURCE=HTTP Request→out TARGET=HTTP Reply→in (backend response)
Tests to generate: TC for "Prepare Request" : input=-0, expected=-1, terminal=out (pure ESQL, no live calls) TC for "HTTP Request" : input=-1, expected=-2, terminal=out (live backend call, use ignorePath)

ibmint_test_commands.overview: IBM App Connect Enterprise v12.0.8+ provides ibmint CLI commands to automate test generation and analysis from recorded messages. These commands run from the IBM App Connect Enterprise Console (not the Toolkit) and require mqsiprofile to be sourced first.

command.name: ibmint generate msgindex
command.description: Analyses recorded messages in a directory and prints a message index showing which nodes were exercised by each .mxml file and the propagation path. Use this BEFORE generating tests to understand coverage and map .mxml → node.
command.examples:
# Analyse recordings from the OndotWorkflow application ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages mmc/OndotWorkflow/
# Analyse recordings on Windows ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages C:\temp\RecordedMessage\mmc\OndotWorkflow\
command.sample_output:
BIP15215I: Found '14' recorded messages in directory 'C:\temp\RecordedMessage\mmc\OndotWorkflow\'. BIP15220I: Generating index from recorded messages.
MessageID: 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001
IIBHUB.ONDOT.INPUT: ; 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-0.mxml Flow Order: 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-0.mxml; 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-1.mxml, ... Store Input Message: 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-1.mxml; 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-2.mxml Load Properties: 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-2.mxml; 00006977-68DD7AF7-00000001-3.mxml ...
command.how_to_read: Each line shows: NodeName: InputCheckpoint; OutputCheckpoint(s) The format is: [NodeLabel]: [input .mxml]; [output .mxml(s)] This tells you exactly which checkpoint pair to use for each node's test case.

command.name: ibmint generate tests
command.description:
PRIMARY method for creating ACE test projects. Automatically generates a complete JUnit 5 Gradle test project from recorded messages. Available in ACE v12.0.8 and above. The generated project is structurally correct and importable into the ACE Toolkit without any manual file creation.
ALWAYS prefer this command over manual project creation. See workflow Step 4.
Source: https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/app-connect/13.0.x?topic=commands-ibmint-generate-tests-command

parameters.parameter: Fully qualified (or relative) path to a directory containing recorded .mxml files. The command searches ALL subdirectories recursively, so you can pass any level: - A single flow dir: recordings/WeatherAPI_App/getCurrentWeather (tests one flow) - An app-level dir: recordings/WeatherAPI_App (tests all flows) Be careful not to pass a very broad path (e.g. /) — it will try to process everything.
Name or path of the directory where the generated test project will be created. Use the GenTest_[version]_[AppName] naming convention.
Fully-qualified Java class name for the generated test class. Example: com.weather.api.test.WeatherAPITest
Output command help information to the console.

prerequisites.prerequisite: Gradle version 8.2.1 or higher must be installed BEFORE running this command. Gradle is NOT bundled with ACE — install from https://gradle.org/install/ The generated project is Gradle-based (build.gradle), NOT Maven (pom.xml).

command.examples:
# Windows — WeatherAPI app-level dir (cmd /c to inherit ACE profile) $cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate tests --recorded-messages `"$cwd\WeatherAPI_App_RecordedMessages_Orig\WeatherAPI_App`" --output-test-project `"$cwd\GenTest_0_WeatherAPI_App`" --java-class com.weather.api.test.WeatherAPITest" 2>&1
# Windows — single flow dir only cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate tests --recorded-messages `"$cwd\WeatherAPI_App_RecordedMessages_Orig\WeatherAPI_App\getCurrentWeather`" --output-test-project `"$cwd\GenTest_0_getCurrentWeather`" --java-class com.weather.api.test.GetCurrentWeatherTest" 2>&1
# Linux / AIX — absolute paths (from IBM docs example) ibmint generate tests \ --recorded-messages ~/Dev/recordings/flow1 \ --output-test-project ~/Dev/tests/Flow1Tests \ --java-class com.my.bar.Flow1Tests
# Linux / AIX — relative paths (run from inside ~/Dev/recordings) ibmint generate tests \ --recorded-messages flow1 \ --output-test-project ../tests/Flow1Tests \ --java-class com.my.bar.Flow1Tests
command.what_is_generated:
GenTest_0_AppName/ ├── .project ← Eclipse project with ACE test natures ├── testproject.descriptor ← References the application under test ├── .classpath ← ACE test framework JARs on classpath ├── .settings/ │ └── org.eclipse.core.resources.prefs ├── build.gradle ← Gradle build descriptor ├── settings.gradle └── src/main/ ├── java/[package]/[ClassName].java ← Generated JUnit 5 test class └── resources/ └── *.mxml ← Copied from recorded messages
The generated Java class contains: - One @Test method per node transition per recorded session - NodeSpy.evaluate() + equalsMessage() assertions for each transition - @AfterEach TestSetup.restoreAllMocks() cleanup

notes.note: The generated project uses Gradle (build.gradle), not Maven. Gradle 8.2.1+ must be installed separately from https://gradle.org/install/ before the command will succeed.

The generated tests are a starting point — always review and enhance them. Add ignorePath() for time-varying HTTP fields, rename methods descriptively, add terminalPropagateCountIs() assertions. See workflow Step 5.
Since AIX does not include Java by default, recorded messages must be copied (via scp) to a Windows development environment that has the ACE Toolkit and Console installed before running this command.
Import into the ACE Toolkit after generation: File → Import → Existing Projects into Workspace (the Toolkit auto-detects the project type from testproject.descriptor).

command.name: ibmint deploy (test project)
command.description: Deploys a test project BAR file to an integration node and server. The application under test must already be deployed to the same server.

command.name: IntegrationServer --test-project
command.description: Runs all tests in a deployed test project on a standalone integration server. This is the command used to execute tests in CI/CD pipelines and automation. The server MUST have start-msgflows set to false so only the test framework runs.

parameters.parameter: --no-nodejs: Disable Node.js runtime (not needed for test execution)
--start-msgflows false: Do not start the application flows — only run tests
--mq-queue-manager-name: Required only if the flows use MQ
--test-project: Name of the test project to run (must match deployed project name)

command.examples:
# Run tests on AIX (OndotWorkflow example) IntegrationServer \ --work-dir /intgmqm/mqsidata/mqsi/components/mmcbroker/servers/mmc \ --no-nodejs \ --start-msgflows false \ --mq-queue-manager-name IVIMMC1 \ --test-project GenTest_OndotWorkflow
# Run tests on Windows (WeatherAPI example — no MQ) IntegrationServer \ --work-dir ace_demo \ --no-nodejs \ --start-msgflows false \ --test-project WeatherAPI_App_Test

important_notes.note: Tests run at the Integration SERVER level, NOT the Integration NODE level. Configurations in node.conf.yaml are NOT applied. Add any required settings (truststores, keystores, etc.) to the server's overrides/server.conf.yaml instead.
Review test results in the console output. Non-zero exit code indicates test failures.

nodestub_api.description:
NodeStub is an ACE Integration Test API that prevents a node from executing its normal logic and from propagating messages to downstream nodes. It is used alongside NodeSpy for whole-flow integration tests to: - Prevent real outbound calls (HTTP requests, MQ writes) during testing - Isolate specific paths through the flow - Mock external service responses
NodeSpy (used in node-level unit tests) observes a node and lets it run. NodeStub prevents a node from running altogether, acting as a stop point.

pattern.name: whole_flow_with_stubs
pattern.description: Standard pattern for a whole-flow integration test that: 1. Uses NodeSpy on the INPUT node to inject a recorded message (replays real input) 2. Places NodeStubs on OUTPUT nodes to intercept messages before they reach queues/services 3. Asserts that the expected messages arrived at the output stubs This tests the ENTIRE flow end-to-end without triggering real external systems.

pattern.name: error_path_whole_flow
pattern.description: Testing the error/catch path in a whole-flow test. Inject a message that triggers the error path and assert the error output.

nodestub_vs_nodespy.description: Key differences between NodeStub and NodeSpy

row.nodestub: Prevent a node from executing at all
Can feed a mock response back into the flow

row.nodespy: Unit test: test what THIS node produces
row.nodestub: Integration test: mock external services, stop output nodes

enterprise_testing_lifecycle.overview: The enterprise testing lifecycle for IBM ACE uses a 5-step process derived from real-world migration projects (v12 → v13). The approach validates that migrated applications behave identically to the original by first running tests against v12 to establish a baseline, then running the same tests against v13.

validation_strategy.description: Two-phase validation approach: 1. Run all tests against the EXISTING (v12) broker — ensure ALL tests pass (baseline) 2. Run the SAME tests against the MIGRATED (v13) application — confirm functional parity Passing both confirms the migration preserved all behaviour within the test scope.

step.title: Record Messages
step.frequency: Once per application (depends on propagation coverage)

requirements.item: Access to the running integration server (AIX or local)
Ability to restart the execution group
Sample messages to invoke the application

steps.step:
Enable recording by configuring RecordedMessageManager in server.conf.yaml (NOT node.conf.yaml — recording config lives at the server level):
ResourceManagers: RecordedMessageManager: recordedMessagePath: 'C:\temp\IntegrationServer\recorded_messages' recordAllMessages: true

Restart the execution group for the config change to take effect:
# Traditional broker commands mqsistart mmcbroker -e mmc # or mqsistop mmcbroker -e mmc
# ibmint commands (v13) ibmint start server mmc --integration-node mmcbroker ibmint stop server mmc --integration-node mmcbroker # or restart the full node ibmint start node mmcbroker ibmint stop node mmcbroker
Invoke the application with representative sample messages to trigger recording
To stop recording, set recordAllMessages: false in server.conf.yaml and restart the execution group.
Recorded .mxml files are stored in the directory specified by recordedMessagePath, organised as: <recordedMessagePath>/<AppName>/<FlowName>/<messageId>-<n>.mxml

step.critical_note: Configure recording in server.conf.yaml ONLY — NOT node.conf.yaml. Tests run at integration server level and only server.conf.yaml is applied.

requirements.item: Note: On AIX, copy recorded messages to Windows via scp first

steps.step:
Run ibmint generate msgindex to understand coverage BEFORE generating tests. Pass the APP-level recordings directory (containing per-flow subdirectories):
# Windows $cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages `"$cwd\recordings\AppName`"" 2>&1
# Linux / AIX source /opt/ibm/ace/13.0.8.0/server/bin/mqsiprofile ibmint generate msgindex --recorded-messages /path/to/recordings/AppName/
Review the output for coverage gaps — record more messages before generating tests if key paths are missing.

Generate the test project using ibmint generate tests. NOTE: Gradle 8.2.1+ must be installed first (https://gradle.org/install/). --recorded-messages is searched recursively — pass app-level for all flows, or a specific flow subdirectory to generate tests for one flow only:
# Windows $cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint generate tests --recorded-messages `"$cwd\recordings\AppName`" --output-test-project `"$cwd\GenTest_0_AppName`" --java-class com.org.app.test.AppTest" 2>&1
# Linux / AIX source /opt/ibm/ace/13.0.8.0/server/bin/mqsiprofile ibmint generate tests \ --recorded-messages /path/to/recordings/AppName/ \ --output-test-project /path/to/GenTest_0_AppName \ --java-class com.org.app.test.AppTest

Compile the test project and create a BAR file using the command line. This is the IBM-documented step 3 in the procedure — it must happen BEFORE deploying. Requires full source code including dependent libraries to be available locally:
# Windows (cmd /c to inherit ACE profile) $cwd = (Get-Location).Path cmd /c "`"C:\Program Files\IBM\ACE\13.0.8.0\server\bin\mqsiprofile.cmd`" && ibmint package --input-path `"$cwd\GenTest_0_AppName`" --output-bar-file `"$cwd\BARfiles\GenTest_0_AppName.bar`" --project GenTest_0_AppName" 2>&1
# Linux / AIX source /opt/ibm/ace/13.0.8.0/server/bin/mqsiprofile ibmint package \ --input-path GenTest_0_AppName \ --output-bar-file BARfiles/GenTest_0_AppName.bar \ --project GenTest_0_AppName

Review and enhance the generated Java test class (see workflow Step 5): add ignorePath() for HTTP Request tests, rename methods, add terminal assertions. NOTE: This is optional per IBM docs — the generated tests can be used as-is for basic regression coverage.

(Optional) Import the generated project into the ACE Toolkit for editing: File → Import → Existing Projects into Workspace. Import the application source code (and dependent libraries) into the Toolkit too. Use the Toolkit to add further tests or extend existing ones.

requirements.item: Full source code including dependent libraries

important_notes.note: The application under test MUST be deployed to the same execution group BEFORE deploying the test project
Access to full source code (including dependent libraries) is required for BAR creation
Deployment order matters: application first, test project second

requirements.item: Access to the integration server environment
Ability to run the IntegrationServer command

notes.note: Only this step (Step 4) needs to be repeated for each migration or release cycle
Steps 1-3 are one-time setup activities per application
If new bugs are discovered, record the triggering messages and generate additional tests (go back to Step 1)

step.title: Automate
step.description: Wrap the IntegrationServer command in a shell script that discovers all test projects by naming convention and runs them all. Hook the script into Ansible for pipeline execution without direct AIX access.
step.ansible_playbook:
--- - name: Run ACE test projects hosts: my_broker_hosts vars: broker_name: "AIXTestNode" eg_name: "AIXTestServer" qm_name: "IVIMMC1" log_mode: "file" # or "console"
tasks: - name: Run all tests via script shell: > /home/cecuser/run_all_tests.sh {{ broker_name }} {{ eg_name }} {{ qm_name }} --log-mode {{ log_mode }} register: test_results ignore_errors: yes
- name: Print test summary output debug: msg: "{{ test_results.stdout }}"

failure.name: timestamp_mismatch
failure.description: The most common JUnit test failure in auto-generated tests. Recorded messages capture timestamps at recording time; when tests re-execute, current system time differs, causing field comparison failures.
failure.example_error: java.lang.AssertionError: Expected: TestMessageAssemblies should have matching message trees. but: Expected value '2025-10-01 15:03:20.104670' but was '2025-10-28 13:49:27.878221' (path: /Message/XMLNSC/db_record_data/createdDate)
failure.fix: Use ignorePath() to exclude the time-varying fields from comparison. Navigate to the line referenced in the error (e.g., tests.java:549) and add ignorePath() calls for the affected paths.

failure.name: propagation_count_mismatch
failure.description: Auto-generated tests assume all node propagations happen simultaneously and independently. In real flows with sequential propagation patterns (Node A → Node B waits for Node C to finish before Node A → Node D), the generated test may expect a propagation that has not occurred yet.
failure.example_pattern: Node A propagates out → Node B → Node C (finishes) → returns to Node A → Node A propagates out1 → Node D. The generated test may expect BOTH Node B and Node D to be called simultaneously, failing with propagation count = 0 on Node D.
failure.example_error: java.lang.AssertionError: Expected: Terminal:"out" to have propagate count:1 but: Node "Store Input Message" Terminal:"out" has propagate count:0
failure.fix_option_1: Modify the generated test to remove or adjust the propagation count assertion for the terminal that fires after the sequential execution completes.
failure.recommendation: Propagation count mismatches usually indicate that auto-generated tests are not the right approach for that node. Write a whole-flow integration test instead (Section 10) that tests the complete message propagation lifecycle.

failure.name: ssl_handshake_exception
failure.description: Tests run at the Integration Server level (NOT the Integration Node level). SSL/TLS configurations defined in node.conf.yaml (truststore, keystore, certificates) are NOT applied during test execution. This causes SSLHandshakeException when tests attempt HTTPS connections.
failure.example_error: TestException(BIP3165) BIP3165E: An error occurred whilst performing an SSL socket operation. Operation: connect. Error Text: javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: No X509TrustManager implementation available.
failure.fix:
Copy the required security configuration entries from node.conf.yaml into the execution group's overrides/server.conf.yaml:
# In servers/<execution_group_name>/overrides/server.conf.yaml BrokerRegistry: brokerTruststoreFile: '/path/to/truststore.jks'
failure.rule: Always place security configuration in server.conf.yaml, not node.conf.yaml, when it is needed for test execution.

failure.name: generated_tests_brittle
failure.description: Auto-generated tests are tightly coupled to the recorded messages and are brittle — any change to the application may require regenerating and reimporting tests.

symptoms.symptom: Tests that passed yesterday fail after a minor code change
Large number of ignorePath() calls needed to maintain tests
Tests difficult to understand without reading both .mxml files and generated Java

failure.recommendation: Use generated tests as a starting point and for regression testing during migration. For long-lived test suites, invest in manually written whole-flow integration tests (Section 10) that are more resilient to application changes.

understanding_recorded_messages.description:
A recorded message (.mxml file) represents a snapshot of a message assembly at a specific point in a running message flow. Each file encodes four internal trees: - Message tree: headers and payload - Environment tree: Environment variables set by nodes - Local environment tree: LocalEnvironment (HTTP, WrittenDestination, etc.) - Exception list tree: any exceptions at this point
An additional checkpoint element captures metadata about WHERE in the flow this snapshot was taken.

checkpoint_metadata.field: Unique identifier grouping all checkpoints for one message invocation
Sequential position in the flow (0 = first node output)
Depth of the node call stack when captured
Exact time the checkpoint was recorded

The node that EMITTED the message: - name: node label - type: node type (ComIbmComputeNode, InputNode, etc.) - terminal: which terminal fired (out, failure, out1, etc.) - application, messageFlow, subflow path - inputNode: TRUE if this is an input-type node
The node that will RECEIVE the message: - same fields as source

reading_checkpoints.description: To determine which test a checkpoint supports: - The SOURCE tells you which node PRODUCED the message (the node under test's output) - The TARGET tells you which node will RECEIVE it (which test this feeds into as input) - For node N: use checkpoint[N] as the expected OUTPUT, checkpoint[N-1] as the INPUT

bash_script_for_source_target.description: With many .mxml files, manually reading source/target is tedious. Use this bash script with xmllint to quickly parse all files:
bash_script_for_source_target.code:
#!/bin/bash # parse_mxml.sh — print source and target node for each .mxml file # Requires: xmllint (install: sudo snap install libxml2)
for f in *.mxml; do echo "File: $f" src=$(xmllint --xpath \ "string(//*[local-name()='checkpoint']/*[local-name()='source']/*[local-name()='name'])" \ "$f" 2>/dev/null) tgt=$(xmllint --xpath \ "string(//*[local-name()='checkpoint']/*[local-name()='target']/*[local-name()='name'])" \ "$f" 2>/dev/null) echo " Source: $src" echo " Target: $tgt" echo done
bash_script_for_source_target.installation: Linux: sudo apt-get install libxml2-utils Snap: sudo snap install libxml2 Windows: use xmllint from the libxml2 Windows binary package

mapping_table_example.description: Example output from parse_mxml.sh for getCurrentWeather flow:
mapping_table_example.table:
File: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-0.mxml Source: HTTP Input Target: Prepare Request
File: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-1.mxml Source: Prepare Request Target: HTTP Request
File: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-2.mxml Source: HTTP Request Target: HTTP Reply
Therefore: Test "Prepare Request": input=*-0.mxml expected=*-1.mxml terminal=out Test "HTTP Request": input=*-1.mxml expected=*-2.mxml terminal=out (use ignorePath for live response)

aspect.name: Effort to create
aspect.generated: Low — ibmint generate tests produces all test cases automatically
aspect.manual: High — requires understanding the flow, writing Java, choosing assertions

aspect.name: Test coverage
aspect.generated: Node-by-node: each node transition becomes a test case
aspect.manual: Can test complete end-to-end scenarios with multiple assertions

aspect.name: Brittleness
aspect.generated: High — tightly coupled to recorded messages; any flow change may break tests
aspect.manual: Lower — tests are written against the intent of the flow, not exact byte values

aspect.name: Sequential propagation handling
aspect.generated: Poor — assumes parallel propagation, fails for sequential patterns
aspect.manual: Good — can be written to match actual flow execution order

aspect.name: Maintenance
aspect.generated: Difficult — modifying generated tests requires re-understanding recorded data
aspect.manual: Easier — test intent is self-documenting in Java method names and comments

aspect.name: Recommended use
aspect.generated: Migration testing (v12 → v13) and quick regression baseline
aspect.manual: Long-lived test suites, complex flows, pipelines requiring stability

generated_vs_manual_tests.recommendation: Use generated tests to rapidly establish a regression baseline during migration projects. Invest in manual whole-flow integration tests (NodeSpy + NodeStub) for flows that will be actively maintained and evolved. The two approaches complement each other: generated tests catch regressions; manual tests document business requirements.
